there is a real number $ x $ greater than 1 that is not an integer, and satisfies the inequality:

$$
\{x\}+\left\{x^{3}\right\}+\left\{x^{5}\right\}+\cdots+\left\{x^{99}\right\}<\frac{1}{2^{99}}
$$

where $ \{x\} $ denotes the fractional part of $ x $. the fractional part of a number is the part of the number after the decimal point, and it is always between 0 (inclusive) and 1 (exclusive). for example, $ \{2.5\} = 0.5 $, $ \{3.999\} = 0.999 $, and $ \{1.0\} = 0 $.

the fractional part of $ x $ is important because it helps us understand the behavior of the sequence $ \{x\}, \{x^3\}, \{x^5\}, \ldots, \{x^{99}\} $. each term in the sum is the fractional part of $ x $ raised to an odd power. since $ x > 1 $, $ x^k $ will grow rapidly as $ k $ increases. this means that the values of $ \{x^k\} $ will also grow, but they will always be less than 1.

the key to solving this inequality is understanding how the fractional parts behave as $ x $ increases. for $ x > 1 $, the fractional part of $ x^k $ is always less than 1, and it depends on the fractional part of $ x $ itself. if $ x $ is irrational, then the fractional parts of $ x^k $ will never repeat, and they will be dense in the interval [0, 1). if $ x $ is rational, then the fractional parts will eventually become periodic.
